title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who share their views on social media. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Connected or Consumed? Four People on Social Media

texts.A:
### Tara, 21, Fashion Student
[43]Social media is genuinely central to my studies and my future career – I use it to research trends, build a portfolio, and follow industry professionals.[/43] I'm on several platforms daily and I don't feel guilty about it. [47]The negative side is that it can seriously damage your self-esteem if you're not careful about how you use it.[/47] I spent months comparing myself to people with perfect photos and perfect lives until I realised those images represent about one percent of reality. [51]Now I'm more selective – I follow people who inspire me rather than people who make me feel inadequate, and that has made a real difference.[/51]
texts.B:
### Hugh, 49, Solicitor
[44]I'm not on any social media platforms at all and I have no intention of joining them.[/44] I'm aware this makes me unusual, especially professionally, but I value my privacy too highly. [48]I've watched colleagues become obsessed with their online image and spend hours crafting posts that project a version of themselves that bears little relation to reality.[/48] That seems deeply unappealing to me. [52]My children are on every platform imaginable and I try to have honest conversations with them about what they share and with whom, without being too controlling about it.[/52]
texts.C:
### Lydia, 35, Nurse
[45]I use social media mainly to stay connected with friends and family who live far away, and for that purpose it's wonderful.[/45] My sister emigrated to Australia five years ago and without social media I'm not sure how we would maintain such a close relationship. [49]Where I draw a firm line is sharing anything about my work – patients have a right to complete privacy and the consequences of breaching that can be severe.[/49] [53]I took a break from all platforms for three months last year and I noticed my concentration and my mood both improved significantly.[/53] I'm now much more deliberate about how much time I spend online.
texts.D:
### Hassan, 28, Social Media Manager
I work in social media professionally, which means I have a complicated relationship with it. [46]I spend eight hours a day managing accounts for companies, crafting content designed to maximise engagement, and analysing what makes people click.[/46] [50]Understanding the mechanics of social media from the inside has made me deeply wary of how platforms manipulate users' attention and emotions.[/50] I use personal accounts very sparingly as a result. [54]The most rewarding part of my job is running campaigns for small charities – seeing genuine community impact from a well-targeted post is very satisfying.[/54] It's a reminder that the technology itself isn't the problem.

questions.question: sees social media as a professional necessity directly linked to their field of study or work?
questions.explanation: Tara says social media is central to her studies and future career, using it to research trends and build a portfolio.

questions.question: chooses not to use any social media platforms at all?
questions.explanation: Hugh says he is not on any social media platforms and has no intention of joining them.

questions.question: primarily values social media for helping them stay in touch with people who live far away?
questions.explanation: Lydia uses social media mainly to stay connected with friends and family who live far away.

questions.question: manages social media accounts as their main occupation?
questions.explanation: Hassan works as a social media manager, spending eight hours a day managing company accounts.

questions.question: warns that social media can harm people's feelings about themselves?
questions.explanation: Tara says social media can seriously damage self-esteem if you are not careful about how you use it.

questions.question: has observed others spending a great deal of effort creating a false impression of themselves online?
questions.explanation: Hugh has watched colleagues spend hours crafting posts that project a version of themselves bearing little relation to reality.

questions.question: maintains a strict boundary around what they will and will not share online, related to their work?
questions.explanation: Lydia draws a firm line at sharing anything about her work because patients have a right to complete privacy.

questions.question: has become more cautious about social media as a result of knowing how it works professionally?
questions.explanation: Hassan's insider knowledge of how platforms manipulate attention has made him deeply wary of social media.

questions.question: changed the way they interact with social media after realising it was having a negative effect?
questions.explanation: Tara became more selective about who she follows after spending months feeling inadequate from comparisons with others.

questions.question: tries to guide younger members of their family in using social media responsibly?
questions.explanation: Hugh has honest conversations with his children about what they share online without being too controlling.
